items.title: USDA finalizes new SNAP retailer stocking standards; compliance required by Nov. 4, 2026
items.summary: USDA’s final rule updates the staple food stocking standards for SNAP-authorized retailers. Most retailers (other than specialty stores) must stock at least 7 varieties in each of the 4 staple food categories (dairy; fruits/vegetables; grains; protein) and offer perishable varieties in at least 3 of the 4 categories by Nov. 4, 2026.

items.title: Summer EBT provides $120 per eligible child for summer groceries; some families must apply
items.summary: USDA says Summer EBT provides $120 in grocery benefits per eligible school-age child when school is out for summer. Many children will receive benefits automatically if their household already gets SNAP or TANF, but some families must apply through their state and may need to provide the child’s details plus household income.

items.title: HUD sets HOTMA sections 102 and 104 enforcement date to Jan. 1, 2027 (most PHAs)
items.summary: HUD’s PIH Notice 2026-15 says HUD will enforce compliance with all provisions of HOTMA sections 102 and 104 starting Jan. 1, 2027 for PHAs that are not MTW and not exclusively using HUD Family Reporting Software. PHAs that start annual reexaminations 120 days before the effective date may need to begin using HOTMA policies for income collection/verification as early as Sept. 2026.
items.source: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD)

items.title: USDA final rule expands milk options in child nutrition programs; comments due June 8, 2026
items.summary: USDA issued a final rule expanding fluid milk options in child nutrition programs with an effective date of June 8, 2026. USDA is also accepting comments on the final rule through June 8, 2026.

items.title: Education Dept. finalizes Workforce Pell: short-term programs (8–15 weeks; 150–599 hours) can qualify
items.summary: The U.S. Department of Education says its final rule implements Workforce Pell for short-term, workforce-focused programs lasting at least 8 weeks but less than 15 weeks and totaling 150–599 clock hours (or credit-hour equivalent). The rule’s workforce-program provisions are effective July 20, 2026, with an option for institutions to implement early on July 1, 2026.

items.title: Workforce Pell accountability: 70% completion and 70% employment, plus tuition cap tied to earnings
items.summary: Education’s fact sheet says eligible Workforce Pell programs must meet annual performance thresholds: at least 70% of participants must complete within 150% of normal time, and at least 70% of completers must be employed in the second quarter after exit. The rule also limits programs’ published tuition and fees to not exceed “value-added earnings,” defined as the adjusted median earnings of Pell recipients who complete the program minus 150% of the U.S. poverty guidelines for a single individual.

items.title: SSA says disability claims backlog down 33%, from 1.27M (2024 peak) to 853,000 (April 2026)
items.summary: SSA reported that it reduced the initial disability claims backlog by 33%, from a high of 1.27 million cases in 2024 to 853,000 cases in April 2026. SSA also said it created an SSI Improvement Team to make SSI processing more efficient and help reduce improper payments.

items.title: IRS: Tennessee winter storm victims get tax filing/payment relief (deadline extended to June 8, 2026)
items.summary: The IRS announced disaster tax relief for individuals and businesses in Tennessee affected by Winter Storm Fern, postponing many tax filing and payment deadlines. The IRS says Tennessee taxpayers in all 95 counties now have until June 8, 2026, and the postponement generally covers original or extended deadlines occurring on or after Jan. 22, 2026.

items.title: IRS posts 2026 tax inflation adjustments: standard deduction $32,200 (joint) and $16,100 (single)
items.summary: The IRS published tax-year 2026 inflation-adjusted standard deduction amounts of $32,200 for married couples filing jointly, $16,100 for single filers (and married filing separately), and $24,150 for heads of household. These annual inflation adjustments affect dozens of tax provisions and help set parameters used when families claim credits like the EITC and CTC.
